title: On Fano effect in IR spectra of hydrogenated nanodiamonds
authors: Andrei A. Shiryaev, Evgeni A. Ekimov
abstract: Hydrogenated nanodiamonds may show a "transmission window" in infra-red spectra in the vicinity of diamond Raman frequency. This phenomenon is a manifestation of resonance coupling of incident photons with continuum states (Fano resonance). Hpwever, precise mechanism of appearence of the resonance and of related conductivity - surface hydrogenation or specific type of surface reconstruction - remains debatable. We present detailed analysis of infra-red spectra of nanodiamonds of different sizes (2.6-30 nm) possessing the "transmission window" and show that the C-H stretch vibrations of adsorbed functional groups cannot be responsible the the Fano resonance. At the same time, it is suggested that a bending mode of monohydride termination on nanodiamond (111) face may couple with diamond optical phonon, explaining the Fano resonance in some cases. The relative importance of the monohydride contribution and of the graphitic islets to the appearence of the "transmission window" and conductivity is likely dependent on dominating morphology and size distribution of nanodiamond grains.
summary: This study investigates the origin of the Fano resonance observed in the infrared spectra of hydrogenated nanodiamonds. By analyzing various grain sizes, the authors rule out C-H stretching vibrations as the cause and propose that the resonance arises from the coupling of diamond optical phonons with monohydride bending modes. This provides insight into the surface chemistry and electronic properties of nanodiamond-based materials.
main_problem: Determining the precise physical mechanism behind the Fano resonance (the 'transmission window') and surface conductivity in hydrogenated nanodiamonds.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that C-H stretch vibrations are not responsible for the Fano resonance, suggesting instead that the bending mode of monohydride termination on the (111) face couples with the diamond optical phonon.
method: Infrared (IR) spectroscopy was performed on nanodiamonds of varying sizes (2.6-30 nm) using both transflection and transmission geometries, followed by spectral decomposition and Pearson correlation analysis.
model_or_system: Hydrogenated nanodiamond grains with diameters ranging from 2.6 to 30 nm, featuring surface functional groups and potential graphitic islets.
key_observables: Fano-related absorption dips near the diamond Raman frequency (1332 cm-1), C-H stretching and bending vibrational modes, and surface conductivity.
important_parameters: Nanodiamond grain size (2.6-30 nm), temperature, and the presence of monohydride termination or graphitic domains.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes specific Lorentzian and Gaussian shapes for spectral decomposition and relies on the accuracy of polynomial fits for background subtraction.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows raw IR spectra and spectral decomposition; Fig 2 plots the lack of correlation between C-H and Fano-related bands; Fig 3 and 4 show the inverse relationship between C-H stretches and Fano peaks, and the positive correlation between C-H bending and the Fano component, respectively; Fig 5 shows a specific absorption peak at 2682 cm-1.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Fano resonance problem, describes the synthesis and spectroscopic methods, presents correlation analyses between various vibrational modes, proposes a coupling mechanism involving the (111) face, and discusses the role of grain size and morphology.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
